composed_prompt:
## General
You are a climate policy analyst. Your task is to extract and classify every climate policy from a long policy document.

For each policy, produce a JSON object with these fields:

EXTRACTION FIELDS:
- "role": "individual" | "sub" | "parent"
    • "individual": standalone policy, no parent-child relationship
    • "sub": specific action listed under a named parent initiative (e.g. lettered or numbered list items A., B., C.)
    • "parent": umbrella initiative/program that has sub-actions listed beneath it
- "parent_statement": the name or summary of the parent initiative for "sub" role only. Use null for "individual" and "parent".
- "policy_statement": concise, self-contained commitment summary. Must be supported by source_quote.
- "source_quote": verbatim 2-3 sentence quote from the document. No paraphrasing.
- "section_header": the section or subsection heading this policy appears under, copied from the document.
- "extraction_rationale": why this qualifies as a policy; note any vagueness or edge cases.

CLASSIFICATION FIELDS — assign alongside extraction:

- "primary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions"
    • Mitigation: acts on the climate system (GHG emissions reductions, carbon sinks, renewable energy)
    • Adaptation: responds to climate impacts (resilience, risk reduction, hazard management, cooling)
    • Resource Efficiency: optimises resource use (energy efficiency, water conservation, material use, transport)
    • Nature-Based Solutions: uses ecosystems as climate infrastructure (urban greening, wetlands, reforestation)
    Classify by the primary causal mechanism, not secondary co-benefits.

- "financial_instrument": "yes" | "no"
    • "yes": the policy IS a broad financial instrument — a fee, carbon pricing tool, rebate, grant, subsidy,
      tax credit, loan, trust fund, budget appropriation, or public investment line.
    • "no": the policy is a regulatory, operational, planning, or target-setting commitment.

- "climate_relevance": "direct" | "indirect" | "peripheral"
    • "direct": climate is the primary stated objective — the policy explicitly targets GHG reduction,
      climate adaptation, or climate resilience.
    • "indirect": climate benefit is significant but secondary — the policy would likely exist without
      climate concerns, but its climate impact is substantial and recognized.
    • "peripheral": climate link is distant or speculative — the policy would exist entirely without
      climate concerns.

- "secondary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions" | null
    Assign only when a SECOND distinct climate mechanism is explicitly stated in the policy text or source_quote.
    Do NOT assign based on implied co-benefits or general climate logic. Use this decision table:
    ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │ Primary              │ Type  │ Trigger (must be explicit in text)    │ Secondary        │
    ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    │ Mitigation           │ M-1   │ Named efficiency action as mechanism   │ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Mitigation           │ M-2   │ Named hazard + resilience measure      │ Adaptation       │
    │ Mitigation           │ M-3   │ Biological sequestration as mechanism  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-1  │ Explicit GHG/emissions target stated   │ Mitigation       │
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-2  │ Named climate hazard motivates measure │ Adaptation       │
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-3  │ Ecological mechanism delivers savings  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-1 │ Named climate hazard addressed by NBS  │ Adaptation       │
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-2 │ Carbon sequestration explicitly stated │ Mitigation       │
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-3 │ Resource saving stated as primary goal │ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Adaptation           │ A-1   │ Ecological mechanism delivers adaption │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Adaptation           │ A-2   │ Efficiency measure named in adapt. plan│ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Adaptation           │ A-3   │ Explicit GHG reduction component named │ Mitigation       │
    └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    Common errors to avoid:
    • Do NOT add Mitigation secondary to Resource Efficiency just because efficiency reduces emissions (RE-1 requires an explicit GHG target).
    • Do NOT add NBS secondary to Mitigation just because a city has a tree planting program mentioned elsewhere.
    • Do NOT add Adaptation secondary to every NBS policy — a named hazard must appear in THIS policy's text.
    • Do NOT confuse disaster risk mitigation with GHG mitigation when applying A-3.
    Use null if no secondary mechanism is explicitly stated.

WHAT COUNTS AS A POLICY:
A stated commitment by a governing body to achieve a defined outcome through deliberate action, resource allocation, or regulatory change. Must contain at least one of:
1. Quantifiable target (numbers, units, deadlines)
2. Binding mechanism (ordinance, mandate, regulatory code change)
3. Specific named intervention (program, technology, action)
4. Resource allocation (committed funding)

DO NOT EXTRACT: background information, current conditions, vague aspirations with no concrete anchor, process descriptions without commitments.

HIERARCHY RULES:
- When you see a named initiative followed by lettered or numbered sub-items, extract the initiative header as "parent" AND each sub-item as "sub" with parent_statement pointing to that initiative name.
- If a policy stands alone with no sub-items beneath it, it is "individual".
- When in doubt between "parent" and "individual", prefer "individual" unless there are clear sub-items.

STRATEGY:
1. Process the document section by section using the section headers as anchors.
2. For each section, extract all qualifying policies and assign all classification fields.
3. Collect all extracted policies into a single Python list of dicts.
4. Deduplicate where the same commitment appears multiple times.
5. Store the final list as `all_policies` and call FINAL_VAR(all_policies).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Your final answer MUST be a valid JSON list of policy objects as described above.

## General
You are a climate policy analyst. Extract and classify every *explicit* climate-relevant policy commitment from a long policy document.

For each policy, output a JSON object with these fields:

EXTRACTION FIELDS:
- "role": "individual" | "sub" | "parent"
  • "individual": standalone policy with no clear parent-child structure  
  • "parent": a named umbrella initiative/program/strategy that introduces multiple concrete actions listed directly beneath it  
  • "sub": a specific action/measure listed under a "parent" (e.g., numbered/lettered bullets, distinct subheadings, “will:” action lists)
- "parent_statement": required only for "sub" role; otherwise null. Use the parent initiative name as written (or a tight summary if unnamed).
- "policy_statement": a concise, self-contained commitment summary. Capture who will do what (and by when/how much if stated). Must be directly supported by the source_quote.
- "source_quote": verbatim excerpt (1–3 sentences, or a short bullet plus its lead-in) that contains the commitment. No paraphrasing; include the operative “will/shall/commit” language where possible.
- "section_header": the closest section/subsection heading this policy appears under (copy from the document).
- "extraction_rationale": one sentence explaining why this is a policy (e.g., target, mandate, named program, funding). Note if conditional/aspirational.

CLASSIFICATION FIELDS — assign alongside extraction:
- "primary_category": "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions"  
  Classify by the primary mechanism stated in the quoted text (not implied co-benefits).
- "financial_instrument": "yes" | "no"  
  "yes" only when the policy itself is a fiscal/financial tool or explicit funding commitment (grant, subsidy, rebate, tax, fee, bond, loan, budget allocation, dedicated fund, public investment line).
- "climate_relevance": "direct" | "indirect" | "peripheral"  
  Use "direct" when the text explicitly frames the action as climate/GHG/adaptation/resilience; "indirect" when climate benefit is substantial but not the main stated purpose; "peripheral" only if the climate link is tenuous.
- "secondary_category": "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions" | null  
  Assign only when a second, distinct mechanism is explicitly stated in the same policy text/quote; otherwise null. Do not infer.

WHAT COUNTS AS A POLICY (aim for high recall while staying evidence-bound):
Extract when the text includes a concrete commitment with at least ONE strong anchor:
1) Quantified target (number, %, units, baseline, deadline/timeframe), OR
2) Binding/authoritative mechanism (ordinance, regulation, requirement, standard, prohibition, enforcement), OR
3) Specific named intervention (program, plan to implement X, infrastructure/project, technology deployment, operational change), OR
4) Resource commitment (funding amount, dedicated budget, staffing, procurement/contracting, investment)

Also extract climate plans/strategies/frameworks *when adoption/implementation is committed* (e.g., “adopt/implement/update a Climate Action Plan by 2027”).

DO NOT EXTRACT (avoid spurious items):
- Background/context, problem statements, data, inventories, projections
- Pure aspirations with no action hook (e.g., “support”, “encourage”, “seek to”, “promote” without a specified measure/program/requirement)
- Process-only statements with no deliverable (e.g., “continue to engage stakeholders”) unless tied to producing/adopting a specific plan, rule, or investment
- General principles, goals, or visions that are not attached to a concrete action, mandate, target, or funded program
- Private actions not framed as government commitments

GRANULARITY & DEDUPLICATION:
- Prefer the most atomic actionable unit: if one sentence lists multiple distinct actions, split into separate policies *only if* they could stand alone (different measures or targets).
- If the same commitment is repeated in multiple places, extract it once using the clearest/most specific quote; do not create duplicates.
- If a target and its implementing measure are both explicitly stated, extract both only if they are meaningfully distinct (e.g., “net-zero by 2050” AND “electrify municipal fleet by 2030”).

HIERARCHY RULES:
- Use "parent"+"sub" only when the document clearly presents an umbrella item followed by multiple discrete action items underneath it.  
- If the “parent” is just a descriptive heading with no commitment language, do NOT extract it as a parent; extract only the committed sub-actions.
- When uncertain, prefer "individual" (to avoid spurious parent policies).

WORKFLOW:
1) Move section by section using headings as anchors.
2) Extract every qualifying policy; fill all fields; keep quotes short but complete.
3) Collect into a single Python list of dicts, `all_policies`.
4) Call FINAL_VAR(all_policies).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Return a valid JSON list of policy objects only.

## Mitigation
Extract **Mitigation** policies: actions primarily intended to **reduce greenhouse-gas (GHG) emissions** or **increase/remainably protect carbon sinks**. Extract each distinct policy action even if details are sparse; prioritize capturing the action and sector over perfect quantification.

**What to extract (include):**
- **Targets/commitments** to cut emissions (economy-wide or sectoral), net-zero goals, peaking years, carbon budgets.
- **Energy supply**: renewables mandates/targets, coal phase-out, restrictions on new fossil capacity, clean power standards, grid decarbonization, methane controls in oil & gas.
- **Energy demand/efficiency**: building codes/retrofits, appliance/vehicle efficiency standards, industrial efficiency, demand management.
- **Transport**: EV mandates/incentives, fuel economy standards, modal shift policies, public transit expansion framed as emissions reduction.
- **Industry**: low-carbon manufacturing requirements, process changes, electrification, hydrogen for industry, CCS/CCUS when aimed at CO₂ reduction.
- **Pricing/markets**: carbon taxes, ETS/cap-and-trade, offset rules (only if clearly part of mitigation policy).
- **Non-CO₂**: methane capture, landfill gas, refrigerant (HFC) phase-down, agricultural emissions measures when framed as GHG reduction.
- **Land-use / sinks when mitigation-motivated**: afforestation/reforestation targets, avoided deforestation explicitly for carbon storage, soil carbon programs, blue carbon, peatland protection/restoration when tied to emissions/sinks.
- **Public finance/regulation** explicitly to decarbonize: fossil fuel subsidy reform, green procurement, phase-out of high-emission technologies.

**What NOT to extract (exclude unless explicitly linked to GHG reduction):**
- Pure **adaptation/resilience** measures (flood defenses, drought planning) without emissions/sinks intent.
- Generic **environmental** actions (pollution control, waste cleanliness) unless tied to methane/GHG.
- Pure **biodiversity/conservation** language with no carbon/GHG rationale.
- Vague aspirations (“support green growth”, “promote sustainability”) with no concrete action.
- R&D or “encourage innovation” statements unless connected to a specific mitigation technology/sector action.

**Granularity & deduping:**
- Extract at the **policy-action level**: one entry per concrete instrument/commitment (e.g., “introduce carbon tax”, “ban new coal plants”, “renewable energy target”).
- If multiple actions are listed in one sentence, split into separate policies **only if** they are clearly distinct instruments.
- Merge near-duplicates (same action repeated) into one.

**Minimum evidence threshold (to avoid spurious items):**
- There must be either (a) explicit **mitigation intent** (reduce emissions, decarbonize, net zero, carbon neutrality, GHG, CO₂, methane), or (b) an unambiguous mitigation instrument (ETS, carbon tax, renewable mandate, coal phase-out, efficiency standard, EV mandate, HFC phase-down, methane capture).
- When intent is implied by a clear mitigation instrument, you may extract even without the word “emissions”.

**Record each extracted policy with:**
- **Policy action** (verb + instrument), **sector** (power/transport/buildings/industry/AFOLU/waste/economy-wide), and any **quantifier** (target %, year, coverage, funding) if present.

## Adaptation
Extract **Adaptation** policies: concrete actions whose primary purpose is to **reduce vulnerability or increase resilience to climate hazards** (e.g., heat, flooding, drought, storms, sea-level rise, wildfire smoke) in people, infrastructure, services, ecosystems, or economies.

**Count as Adaptation when the text includes:**
- An explicit climate-risk framing (“climate change”, “climate impacts”, “resilience”, “adaptation”, “risk reduction”) **or** a clear hazard/resilience action strongly linked to climate impacts (e.g., flood protection, drought preparedness, heat health plans).
- A **policy instrument**: requirement/standard, plan/strategy, program, investment, funding, capacity building, warning system, monitoring system, governance reform, or nature-based measure explicitly for risk reduction.

**Include the following common adaptation types (extract as separate policies when distinct):**
- **Risk assessment & planning:** climate risk/vulnerability assessments; adaptation plans; sectoral resilience plans; climate-proofing guidelines; integration of climate risk into budgeting, permitting, land-use, or infrastructure planning.
- **Infrastructure & built environment resilience:** flood defenses/levees, stormwater upgrades, drainage, coastal protection/managed retreat, building codes for heat/flood/wind, resilient roads/bridges/ports, backup power for critical facilities.
- **Water & drought resilience:** water conservation mandates for drought response, diversified supply (reuse/desalination) framed as drought/climate resilience, drought contingency plans, reservoir operations changes, groundwater recharge for drought resilience.
- **Heat & health:** heat action plans, cooling centers, urban heat mitigation framed for heat risk, early warning for heat waves, public health preparedness for climate-related disease/smoke.
- **Disaster risk management:** early warning systems, evacuation planning, emergency preparedness upgrades tied to climate extremes; climate-informed hazard mapping.
- **Ecosystem/land adaptation:** wildfire risk reduction/forest resilience framed as climate resilience, invasive species management for climate impacts, climate-resilient habitat corridors, coastal wetland restoration explicitly for storm surge/SLR protection.
- **Agriculture & livelihoods:** climate-resilient crops, irrigation efficiency when framed as drought resilience, advisory services for climate risks, insurance schemes explicitly for climate extremes.

**Exclude (do not extract as Adaptation):**
- Pure **mitigation** measures aimed mainly at reducing GHGs (renewables, energy efficiency, EVs) unless the text clearly states a resilience/hazard objective.
- Generic “sustainability”, “environmental protection”, “development”, or “innovation” statements without a climate-risk/resilience action.
- Routine disaster response or infrastructure maintenance with no climate/extreme-weather/resilience linkage.
- Emissions inventories, net-zero targets, carbon pricing.

**Granularity & precision rules:**
- Extract **one policy per distinct instrument + objective + sector/hazard**. Split items if they apply to clearly different hazards/sectors or specify different instruments (e.g., “heat early warning” vs “flood early warning”).
- Avoid paraphrasing as a policy if the text is only a problem statement or aspiration; require a verb indicating action (e.g., “establish”, “implement”, “require”, “fund”, “upgrade”, “adopt”, “develop”).
- Keep wording close to the source: include the **who/what**, **hazard or climate rationale**, and **instrument**; include targets/dates if present.

## Resource Efficiency
Extract **Resource Efficiency** policies: measures that reduce material, energy, water, or land throughput per unit of service/output by improving efficiency, circularity, and demand-side management (excluding pure renewables build-out unless explicitly framed as efficiency).

Count an item as a policy only if the text indicates a **government/authority action** (law, regulation, standard, mandate, program, funding, incentive, target, strategy, procurement rule) that is **implemented or clearly proposed**. Prefer explicit instruments and sectors; avoid vague aspirations.

Include (extract as separate policies when distinct):
- **Energy efficiency**: building codes/retrofit programs, appliance/equipment standards & labeling, industrial efficiency requirements, audits/ISO 50001, CHP/waste-heat recovery, efficient streetlighting/public facilities.
- **Transport efficiency / demand management**: fuel economy/CO₂ standards, vehicle inspection/maintenance for efficiency, logistics optimization mandates, eco-driving programs when institutionalized, modal shift programs framed as efficiency (not general “promote public transport” unless tied to reducing energy use).
- **Water efficiency**: leakage reduction programs, efficient irrigation (drip/scheduling), water-use standards, metering & tiered tariffs designed to reduce demand, reuse/greywater requirements when framed as saving freshwater.
- **Material efficiency & circular economy**: waste prevention, reuse/repair, recycling targets/mandates, extended producer responsibility, packaging reduction standards, recycled-content requirements, right-to-repair, industrial symbiosis programs.
- **Resource-efficient agriculture/industry**: fertilizer/nutrient use efficiency programs, precision agriculture aimed at reducing inputs, process optimization mandates.
- **Government procurement**: green/efficient procurement requirements (energy-efficient products, recycled content) when specified.
- **Quantified targets**: e.g., “reduce energy intensity by X% by year Y,” “cut water losses to X%,” “increase recycling rate to X%.”

Exclude (do NOT extract unless explicitly tied to resource-use efficiency/circularity):
- Pure **renewable energy deployment** (solar/wind buildout), general “decarbonize power,” or fossil fuel phase-out (Mitigation).
- General **emissions targets** without an efficiency/circularity mechanism.
- **Adaptation-only** actions (flood defenses, early warning) unless explicitly about conserving resources (e.g., water-saving drought management).
- Broad statements like “promote sustainability/green growth/innovation” without a concrete efficiency instrument.
- Pure **waste treatment** capacity (incinerators/landfills) unless coupled to prevention/reuse/recycling mandates.

Extraction guidance:
- Capture the **instrument + sector + key requirement/target + timeline** if present.
- Split policies when different instruments or sectors are described; combine only when clearly one program with multiple components.
- Use the document’s wording; avoid inferring mechanisms not stated.

## Nature-Based Solutions
Extract **Nature-Based Solutions (NbS)** policies: actions that **protect, restore, sustainably manage, or create ecosystems** to address climate change (mitigation and/or adaptation) and/or deliver co-benefits (biodiversity, water regulation, disaster risk reduction).

**Include a policy when the text specifies** an intended action plus a target ecosystem/land use, such as:
- **Forest/trees:** avoided deforestation; afforestation/reforestation; forest landscape restoration; community forestry; agroforestry; urban forestry/street trees; forest fire prevention when tied to ecosystem management.
- **Wetlands/peatlands:** peatland rewetting; wetland restoration/creation; mangrove restoration; marsh/lake/riverine wetland protection.
- **Coasts/marine:** coral reef restoration; seagrass/kelp restoration; dune restoration; living shorelines; coastal habitat protection for storm surge/erosion.
- **Grasslands/rangelands:** grassland restoration; rotational/managed grazing to restore vegetation and soils; prairie restoration.
- **Watersheds/rivers:** riparian buffers; floodplain reconnection; river restoration; catchment restoration for flood control/water security.
- **Soils/natural carbon:** soil organic carbon enhancement through ecological practices (cover crops, reduced tillage) **only if framed as ecosystem/land restoration or natural carbon sinks**.
- **Protected areas & biodiversity:** expansion/strengthening of protected areas, ecological corridors, habitat connectivity, conservation set-asides **when linked to climate outcomes** (carbon sinks, resilience, risk reduction).
- **Urban NbS:** green roofs/walls, parks, wetlands, permeable green infrastructure **when explicitly described as nature-based/ecosystem solutions** for heat, flooding, or carbon.

**Extraction unit (granularity):**
- Extract **one policy per distinct intervention** (e.g., “mangrove restoration” separate from “coral reef restoration” if both appear).
- If multiple ecosystems share the **same instrument and intent** in one phrase (e.g., “restore wetlands and peatlands”), you may extract **one combined policy**.
- Capture any **quantitative target, location, timeframe, funding, or implementing instrument** (e.g., hectares restored, % forest cover, named program).

**Exclude / do NOT extract as NbS:**
- Purely **engineered/grey infrastructure** (seawalls, dams) unless explicitly paired with “living shoreline”/ecosystem restoration as the core measure.
- **Generic environmental goals** without an action (e.g., “protect biodiversity” with no concrete measure).
- Standard **pollution control, waste, recycling, renewable energy, efficiency** measures (not NbS).
- **Agricultural productivity** measures that are not ecosystem restoration/management (e.g., fertilizer optimization) unless explicitly framed as restoring soils/ecosystems or enhancing natural carbon sinks.
- **Forestry/land** statements that are only about timber production or administrative restructuring with no conservation/restoration action.

**Key cues:** “ecosystem-based,” “nature-based,” “restoration,” “rewetting,” “afforestation/reforestation,” “avoided deforestation,” “habitat,” “mangrove,” “peatland,” “wetland,” “riparian buffer,” “floodplain,” “green infrastructure,” “living shoreline,” “ecological corridor,” “carbon sink.”

## General
You are a climate policy analyst. Your task is to extract and classify climate policies from a long policy document, maximizing coverage while avoiding spurious “policies” that are merely context, plans, or descriptions.

For each extracted policy, produce a JSON object with these fields:

EXTRACTION FIELDS:
- "role": "individual" | "sub" | "parent"
    • "individual": standalone policy/commitment
    • "sub": a specific action that is explicitly listed under a named parent initiative (e.g., numbered/lettered bullets)
    • "parent": an umbrella initiative/program ONLY when it clearly introduces a list of actionable sub-items beneath it
- "parent_statement": the name/summary of the parent initiative for "sub" only; otherwise null.
- "policy_statement": a concise, self-contained commitment that can be understood without extra document context. Must be directly supported by source_quote.
- "source_quote": verbatim quote that contains the commitment language itself (typically 1–3 sentences; may include a bullet item). No paraphrasing.
- "section_header": the closest section/subsection heading for where the quote appears.
- "extraction_rationale": briefly state why it qualifies as a policy and note any uncertainty.

CLASSIFICATION FIELDS — assign alongside extraction:
- "primary_category": "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions"
- "financial_instrument": "yes" | "no"
- "climate_relevance": "direct" | "indirect" | "peripheral"
- "secondary_category": "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions" | null
  Assign secondary_category ONLY if the second mechanism is explicitly stated in the quoted text for THIS policy (not inferred co-benefits). Otherwise null.

WHAT COUNTS AS A POLICY (tightened to reduce false positives):
Extract ONLY when the text makes a clear commitment by a governing body/agency to do/implement/adopt/fund/require something. The quote must include at least ONE of the following concrete anchors:
1) A required or adopted instrument: ordinance, resolution, regulation, code/standard, mandate, requirement, enforcement, formal adoption.
2) A funded/authorized action: budget appropriation, grant/loan/subsidy, dedicated funding, procurement commitment, staffed program launch.
3) A specific implementation action: build/install/retrofit/upgrade/phase out/ban/require/expand/operate/maintain (not just “support/encourage/explore”).
4) A measurable target with a deadline/baseline (e.g., % by year; tons CO2e by year; fleet electrification by year).

DO NOT EXTRACT (common spurious types):
- Background, findings, “whereas” clauses, vision statements, values, or general commitments with no implementable action.
- Descriptions of existing conditions or what a plan/document “includes,” “guides,” “outlines,” or “identifies,” unless the same passage clearly commits to implement specific actions/requirements.
- Pure planning/process steps (e.g., “develop a map,” “create a report,” “study,” “assess,” “consider,” “explore,” “coordinate,” “engage stakeholders”) UNLESS explicitly tied to a mandated decision or implementation timeline that leads to a concrete policy change (e.g., “publish by X date and adopt/update code/standards accordingly”).
- References to a policy being “aligned with” or “consistent with” goals, without a stated action/requirement.
- Aspirational targets with no actor, mechanism, or deadline.

HIERARCHY RULES:
- Use "parent" ONLY when a named initiative is immediately followed by a list of distinct actionable commitments that you will extract as "sub".
- Do NOT create a "parent" for a narrative paragraph that merely discusses a plan/strategy; in such cases extract only the specific actionable commitments (if any) as "individual".
- Use "sub" only when the sub-item is itself an actionable commitment (not a topic label).
- When unsure between "parent" and "individual", prefer "individual" unless the document structure clearly shows sub-items.

DEDUPLICATION / GRANULARITY:
- Extract at the “commitment” level: one object per distinct action/requirement/target.
- If the same commitment appears multiple times, keep the clearest/most specific instance and drop duplicates.
- Do not split a single requirement into multiple near-identical policies; do split when different actions/targets are listed as separate bullets.

STRATEGY:
1) Process section by section using headers as anchors.
2) Extract all qualifying policies under each section; avoid process-only items unless they compel implementation.
3) Collect into one Python list of dicts, deduplicate, store as `all_policies`, and call FINAL_VAR(all_policies).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Return a valid JSON list of policy objects as described above.

## Mitigation
Extract **Mitigation** policies only when the document states a **concrete government action intended to reduce greenhouse-gas emissions or increase carbon sequestration** (energy, buildings, transport, waste, industry, fuels, electricity supply, carbon removal).

**Count as Mitigation (extract as a policy) only if at least one is explicit:**
- A **binding or formal instrument**: ordinance, code, regulation, standard, requirement, mandate, adopted plan/program with defined measures, procurement rule, budgeted project, or executed agreement.
- A **specific commitment** with an action verb (“shall/will/require/implement/phase out/adopt/enforce/fund/build/retrofit/electrify”) tied to an emissions-reducing measure.
- A **quantified target and/or deadline** (e.g., % reduction, net-zero by year, EV adoption by year, building performance threshold, renewable share).
- A **clearly described measure** that is inherently mitigation even without numbers (e.g., ban on fossil-fuel heating in new buildings; building performance standard; fleet electrification mandate; renewable procurement requirement; methane capture requirement).

**Do NOT extract (common false positives):**
- Purely **symbolic statements**: resolutions “affirming commitment,” declarations, aspirational goals, endorsements, visions, or “support/encourage/recognize” language **without** a specified implementing action or requirement.
- **Informational/analytical deliverables** only: mapping, studies, assessments, inventories, outreach, toolkits, dashboards, strategy exploration—unless the document also commits to implementing the resulting infrastructure/policy.
- Descriptions of **existing context** with no change action (“the code includes…”, “the plan guides…”) unless the text states adoption/enforcement/update and what it requires.
- Vague mentions of “reduce emissions” without sector/mechanism, responsible actor, or implementable measure.

**Granularity rules (to avoid spurious splits/merges):**
- Extract **one policy per distinct measure/instrument** (e.g., “adopt building performance standard” vs “electrify municipal fleet” are separate).
- If a plan is mentioned, extract it **only if** the text states it is **adopted/approved** and includes **specific mitigation actions/requirements**; otherwise treat as background.
- Prefer the **most specific operational statement** (measure + who + where + when). Do not restate broad commitments if a concrete action is present.

**What to capture in the policy text:**
- Instrument/type (ordinance/code/standard/plan/program/project), sector, action, covered scope (municipal vs community-wide; building types; fleet), and any targets/timelines.

## Adaptation
Extract **Adaptation** policies: actions whose primary purpose is to **reduce vulnerability** to climate hazards or **increase resilience** of people, infrastructure, ecosystems, or services under current/future climate impacts (e.g., heat, drought, flood, sea-level rise, storms, wildfire smoke).

**What to include (count as a policy if it is a concrete action/measure):**
- **Risk assessment & planning:** climate risk/vulnerability assessments; climate-proofing standards; adaptation plans/strategies; scenario planning explicitly tied to climate hazards.
- **Physical resilience measures:** flood defenses (levees, seawalls), stormwater upgrades, drainage expansion, coastal protection, slope stabilization, wildfire buffers, cooling infrastructure, water storage/drought resilience infrastructure.
- **Nature-based adaptation** when framed for risk reduction (mangroves/wetlands for flood buffering, urban trees for heat): include here if the stated goal is resilience/impact reduction.
- **Public health & safety:** heat-health action plans, cooling centers, early warning systems, smoke shelters, disease/vector surveillance tied to climate.
- **Water & agriculture resilience:** drought contingency plans, irrigation efficiency explicitly for drought resilience, climate-resilient crops/varieties, shifting planting calendars when linked to climate impacts.
- **Operations & preparedness:** emergency response improvements, disaster risk management measures explicitly linked to climate change/extreme weather; resilient supply chains for critical services.
- **Policy instruments:** building codes/land-use rules for flood/heat risk, managed retreat/relocation programs, insurance/reinsurance reforms **only when explicitly linked to climate risk/adaptation**.
- **Finance & governance:** dedicated adaptation funding, resilience bonds, adaptation mainstreaming requirements in agencies—if tied to adaptation outcomes.

**What NOT to include (avoid spurious extractions):**
- Pure **mitigation** (renewables, efficiency, EVs, carbon pricing) unless the text explicitly states an adaptation/resilience purpose.
- Generic “sustainability,” “environmental protection,” “disaster management,” or “infrastructure modernization” **without explicit climate-hazard/resilience framing**.
- Routine maintenance or general social programs unless clearly positioned as climate adaptation.
- Broad research/education/awareness with no stated adaptation application or implementable measure.

**Extraction rules (to improve matching quality):**
- Extract policies at the **measure level** (one action per policy). Split lists into separate items when they are distinct measures.
- Keep wording **close to the source**; include any **target sector/hazard**, **location**, and **instrument** (plan, standard, investment, program).
- If a sentence is ambiguous, only extract if it clearly references **climate impacts/hazards**, **resilience**, **adaptation**, or **climate-proofing**.

## Resource Efficiency
Extract **Resource Efficiency** policies: measures that **reduce energy/material/water use per unit of service/output**, **reduce waste**, or **increase reuse/recycling/circularity** in production, buildings, infrastructure, products, or public services.

**Include (count as a policy) only when the text specifies an actual measure**, such as:
- **Efficiency improvements** (energy efficiency in buildings/industry/appliances; process optimization; loss reduction; high-efficiency equipment; audits/retrofits mandated or funded).
- **Demand reduction / conservation** (water conservation programs; leakage reduction; load management framed as efficiency; procurement standards requiring efficient products).
- **Material efficiency / circular economy** (design for durability/repair; reuse; remanufacturing; recycling targets/mandates; extended producer responsibility; waste sorting/collection tied to recycling; reducing packaging/material intensity).
- **Waste prevention and diversion** (landfill diversion requirements; food waste reduction programs; composting programs when framed as waste reduction).
- **Resource productivity standards/benchmarks** (intensity targets like kWh/m², water-use intensity, material-use intensity) and **implementation instruments** (standards, bans, incentives, financing, reporting) tied to the above.

**Extract each distinct policy separately** when they differ by sector (buildings vs industry), resource (energy vs water vs materials), or instrument (standard vs subsidy vs program).

**Exclude (avoid spurious extraction):**
- Pure **renewable energy supply expansion** (solar/wind/hydrogen) unless explicitly framed as **efficiency/demand reduction**.
- **Emissions targets**, carbon pricing, ETS, or general “decarbonization/green transition” statements without a clear efficiency/circular action.
- Generic aspirations (“promote efficiency”, “support circular economy”, “encourage recycling”) **without a concrete mechanism, program, mandate, target, or defined action**.
- Pure **pollution control/end-of-pipe** (filters, scrubbers) unless linked to resource savings.
- **Land-use, forestry, biodiversity, ecosystem restoration** (belongs to Nature-Based Solutions).
- **Adaptation-only** measures (flood defenses, drought response) unless specifically about **water efficiency/leak reduction/conservation**.

**Evidence threshold:** The passage must contain a **verb indicating action** (mandate/require/ban/implement/establish/fund/introduce/expand) **and** a specific efficiency/circular/waste-reduction content (technology, standard, program, target, or enforcement). If it’s only high-level intent with no actionable detail, do not extract.

## Nature-Based Solutions
Extract **Nature-Based Solutions (NBS)** policies: actions that **protect, restore, sustainably manage, or create ecosystems** to deliver climate mitigation/adaptation co-benefits (carbon sequestration, flood/heat buffering, drought resilience, coastal protection) and/or biodiversity benefits.

Include as NBS when the text specifies one or more of the following (count each distinct policy/action separately):
- **Forest ecosystems**: afforestation/reforestation, forest restoration, avoided deforestation, REDD+/REDD, improved forest management, community/indigenous forest stewardship tied to conservation/restoration outcomes.
- **Peatlands and wetlands**: peatland rewetting, wetland restoration/creation, marsh/mangrove conservation or restoration, riparian buffer restoration.
- **Coastal/marine ecosystems**: mangroves, seagrass, saltmarsh restoration/conservation (“blue carbon”), living shorelines, dune restoration, coral reef restoration when framed as nature-based coastal protection.
- **Grasslands/savannas/rangelands**: grassland restoration, sustainable rangeland management aimed at ecosystem recovery or soil carbon, native habitat restoration.
- **Urban nature-based measures**: urban tree canopy expansion, green corridors, green roofs/walls, parks/wetlands for stormwater retention or heat reduction—only if the nature component is explicit.
- **Watershed/catchment restoration**: catchment rehabilitation, river/floodplain restoration, natural water retention measures, headwater protection for flood/drought regulation.
- **Soil/ecosystem restoration**: erosion control via vegetative measures, agroforestry or silvopasture **when explicitly framed as ecosystem restoration/trees-in-landscapes for climate/biodiversity**.
- **Protected areas/conservation**: establishing/expanding protected areas, ecological networks, habitat conservation **when tied to preventing ecosystem loss or enhancing ecosystem services** relevant to climate/biodiversity.

Do **not** extract as NBS (avoid spurious):
- Purely **technological/engineering** measures without ecosystem restoration (e.g., seawalls, drainage pipes, desalination) unless explicitly “nature-based/living shoreline/green infrastructure”.
- Generic **awareness, research, capacity building, monitoring, mapping, strategies, plans** unless they commit to a concrete on-the-ground NBS action (e.g., “restore X hectares of mangroves”).
- **Agricultural productivity** measures (fertilizer efficiency, irrigation upgrades) unless clearly tied to ecosystem restoration (e.g., restoring riparian buffers, agroforestry planting).
- **Waste, energy, transport, industry** actions unless they directly involve ecosystems (e.g., landfill gas capture is not NBS).

Extraction guidance:
- Capture the **specific action + ecosystem type + location/scale/target** if stated (e.g., hectares, coastline length, number of trees, protected area size, year).
- If multiple ecosystems/actions are listed, extract **separate policies** when they are distinct commitments (e.g., “restore wetlands” and “expand protected forests”).
- Prefer **verbatim, concrete phrasing** from the text; avoid adding inferred goals.
